text.title: Resource-Oriented Design, HTTP Methods, Status Codes
text.summary: Path naming conventions, correct HTTP method semantics, required status codes, and the most common anti-patterns agents produce.

rule.statement: Paths use plural nouns in kebab-case; no verbs; singletons nested under their collection: /users/{id}/orders/{order-id}.
rule.rationale: Verb paths (/getUsers, /doThing) couple the URL to implementation, break caching semantics, and confuse SDK generators. Plural nouns make collection vs singleton unambiguous.

example.code: GET /users # list GET /users/{id} # get one POST /users # create PATCH /users/{id} # partial update PUT /users/{id} # full replace DELETE /users/{id} # delete GET /users/{id}/orders # sub-collection

antipattern.reason: Verbs in paths make REST APIs RPC-shaped, breaking HTTP caching, client SDK generation, and gateway routing rules.

section.title: HTTP Method Semantics

rule.statement: Use PATCH for partial updates and PUT for full replacement; most CRUD operations want PATCH. GET and DELETE must be idempotent.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to PUT for every update; this silently NULLs fields the client didn't send. PATCH with a partial body is safe for incremental edits.

section.p: Method table: GET = read, safe + idempotent. POST = create, not safe/idempotent. PUT = replace, idempotent. PATCH = partial update. DELETE = remove, idempotent.

section.title: Status Codes

rule.statement: POST that creates a resource must return 201 + Location header. Successful DELETE returns 204. Never use 200 for creation or deletion.
rule.rationale: 200 on creation hides whether the resource is new or existing; clients can't distinguish. Location on 201 gives the new resource URI without a second request.

section.p: Core codes: 200 GET/PUT/PATCH success. 201 POST success. 204 DELETE success. 400 validation. 401 missing/invalid auth. 403 forbidden. 404 not found. 409 conflict. 422 semantic validation. 429 rate limit. 500 server error.

rule.statement: Return errors as RFC 7807 application/problem+json so clients and LLM agents parse a stable structure.
rule.rationale: Custom error shapes per endpoint require endpoint-specific parsing. RFC 7807 gives a known shape (type, title, status, detail) that clients handle generically.
